description: 'sahu' covers a memory fading over years or a name unavailable when it is needed. It says that something is no longer retained or cannot be brought to mind. If an act of letting go matters, 'leiro' (release) can state it; forgetting itself has no built-in intention.
articulatory_notes: Hold the tongue close to the alveolar ridge and direct voiceless air through a narrow groove for 's', then open into stressed central 'a'. Let unvoiced air pass through the open glottis for 'h' and finish with high-back rounded 'u'.
sound_symbolism: The hiss of 's' opens into 'a' before the narrow tongue channel gives way to the breath of 'h'. In Phi, that loosening may recall a memory slipping out of reach as the word rounds into 'u'.
usage_notes: The forgotten content comes before the verb as its direct object. Passive 'se sahu' presents something as forgotten; inchoative 'pa sahu' marks the beginning of forgetting. In noun position, 'sahu' is forgetting or a lapse of memory.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Memory changes and fails with the rest of experience. 'sahu' names a lapse in memory, not a moral defect.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Plain 'sahu' distinguishes a forgotten promise or name from deliberate refusal or deception. Repair can begin with what actually happened.

semantic_domains.cognition: something no longer retained or presently available in memory
semantic_domains.temporal: memory becoming unavailable as time and experience change
